coredata.dc:title: Oilothermal, a novel pyrolysis method for fabrication of carbon dots-loaded carriers from cyanobacteria for use in solid-phase extraction of cadmium
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Khavari Farid, Reza
Rahimi, Ghasem
coredata.dc:description:
Background Making carbon dots-loaded carriers (CLCs) required the use of two types of precursors (one for carrier particles and the other for carbon dots) and at least three steps. In this study, CLCs were fabricated by using cyanobacterial cells as a dual-purpose precursor and oilothermal as an innovative pyrolysis method.
Methods The oilothermal method allowed that each cyanobacterial cell (carrier particle precursor) with its hydrous exopolysaccharides (EPSs) layer (carbon dots precursor) to be encapsulated together by an oil micelle and pyrolyzed separate from other cells and formed the CLC in one step. The potential of emerging CLCs for solid-phase extraction (SPE) was assessed. For this purpose, the CLCs were tested in a cadmium-based model.
Significant findings The range of the maximum pH-dependent adsorption efficiency (>95%) was extensive (pH 4–10). The adsorption kinetics and isotherm had the best fit with the pseudo-second-order and Langmuir linear models, respectively. The adsorption capacity was 5305.61 mg kg−1 only in 2 min exposure time. The findings in this research conclude that emerging CLCs, because of their unique features such as high capacity, fast adsorption rate, fast recovery rate, green origin, optimal and efficient fabrication method, have a high potential for SPE of trace cadmium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: oilothermal
pyrolysis
cyanobacteria
carbon dots
cadmium

Approval of EPSs as the origin of CDs
Optical and scanning electron microscopy imaging
Optical and scanning electron microscopy imaging
The recovery of cadmium from CLCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Cyanobacteria cell suspension; (b) CLCs suspension; and (c) 100x magnification of CLCs suspension (taken by a digital pen microscope).

Fluorescent luminosities: (a) cyanobacteria cell suspension; (b) CLCs suspension; (c) CFCs suspension; (d) the FCs suspension; (e) the fresh frying oil; (f) heated frying oil; (g) acetone; (h) fluorescence microscope image of cyanobacteria cells; and (i) CLCs.

Microscopic images: (a) and (e) optical microscopy images of Aphanocapsa sp. IBRC-M 5171 cells in two magnifications; (b) and (f) optical microscope images of CLCs-CFCs in two magnifications; (c) and (g) SEM images of CLCs in two magnifications; (d) and (h) SEM images of CFCs in two magnifications; (i) SEM images of CLCs surface; (j) SEM images of CFC surface; (k) SEM image of FCs thick sample; (l) SEM image of FCs diluted sample; and (m) the size distribution of FCs.
(a) pH-dependent cadmium adsorption on CLCs; (b) adsorption kinetic in cadmium standard solutions; and (c) adsorption isotherm.
Dependence of adsorption efficiency on co-existing cations.
Evaluations of elution efficiency: (a) Dependence of elution efficiency on the eluent concentration; and (b) dependence of elution efficiency on the eluent volume.

Adjusted pH by 0.1 M HCl and NaOH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of cadmium in the presence of co-existing cations (Mean ± S.D.).

head.ce:title: Oilothermal, a novel pyrolysis method for fabrication of carbon dots-loaded carriers from cyanobacteria for use in solid-phase extraction of cadmium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Soil Science Department, Faculty of Agriculture, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Soil Science Department, Faculty of Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Soil Science Department, Faculty of Agriculture, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Making carbon dots-loaded carriers (CLCs) required the use of two types of precursors (one for carrier particles and the other for carbon dots) and at least three steps. In this study, CLCs were fabricated by using cyanobacterial cells as a dual-purpose precursor and oilothermal as an innovative pyrolysis method.

The oilothermal method allowed that each cyanobacterial cell (carrier particle precursor) with its hydrous exopolysaccharides (EPSs) layer (carbon dots precursor) to be encapsulated together by an oil micelle and pyrolyzed separate from other cells and formed the CLC in one step. The potential of emerging CLCs for solid-phase extraction (SPE) was assessed. For this purpose, the CLCs were tested in a cadmium-based model.

The range of the maximum pH-dependent adsorption efficiency (>95%) was extensive (pH 4–10). The adsorption kinetics and isotherm had the best fit with the pseudo-second-order and Langmuir linear models, respectively. The adsorption capacity was 5305.61 mg kg−1 only in 2 min exposure time. The findings in this research conclude that emerging CLCs, because of their unique features such as high capacity, fast adsorption rate, fast recovery rate, green origin, optimal and efficient fabrication method, have a high potential for SPE of trace cadmium.

Oilothermal, a novel pyrolysis method for fabrication of carbon dots-loaded carriers from cyanobacteria for use in solid-phase extraction of cadmium
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Cadmium is a hazardous element in nature; it has no role in the body and has harmful effects on the organs [1–5]. The biological half-life of cadmium in the liver and kidneys are estimated to be between 4 and 19 and 6 to 38 years, respectively. Accordingly, if the body is exposed to even insignificant cadmium concentrations, it can be problematic in the long term, and so ultra-trace levels of cadmium should be monitored in the environment [6].

Measurement of trace cadmium concentrations in natural samples requires a preconcentration treatment before analysis [6]. Nowadays, general techniques are widely used for preconcentration, including solid-phase extraction (SPE), cloud point extraction, liquid-liquid extraction, and liquid-liquid microextraction [6–10]. Among these, the SPE is a widely used technique because of its relatively convenient operation, cheapness, safety, less solvent consumption, and high extraction efficiency [6, 11-13].

Various materials such as carbonaceous materials, mesoporous materials, magnetic materials, and molecular or ion-imprinted polymers have been widely used in SPE [6, 14-19]. Nowadays, the use of carbon nanomaterials has expanded due to their unique features such as easy physical and chemical modification, high surface area, ability to adjust properties for specific applications, high capacity for microbial refining, and removal of biological and chemical pollutions from the water environments [20-21]. Carbon nanomaterials are classified into four groups of zero-, one-, two- and three-dimensional nanostructures [6].

Carbon dots (CDs) are from the group of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures [6, 22]. They have the smallest size among other carbon nanostructures groups, and their size is less than 10 nm [23]. The CDs have fluorescent properties and can emit light in various colors [26]. Today, there is a high tendency to use CDs in applied fields such as adsorption, because of their unique features such as easy and cheap fabrication methods, optical and thermal resistance, facile surface functionalization, non-toxicity, and biocompatibility [24–27]. But it is challenging to separate CDs from the water environment because of their high hydrophilicity and their tiny size that makes it almost impossible to separate them by centrifugation [6]. Hence, CDs must be fixed on a microcarrier before use [6].

The use of CDs in the SPE is a new and emerging approach so that very little research has been done in this area. according to a review of recent literature, the latest and most relevant study in this context was Li et al. (2018) [6]. They have used cytopore beads as carriers of CDs in cadmium SPE [6]. Cytopores are micrometric porous cellulose beads that are used as a substrate for culturing anchorage-dependent animal cells [28]. Cytopore is an expensive microcarrier that costs about 1000 USD for every 20 g (Exactly 948.00 USD on 04/01/2021) [29]. Therefore, it is economically better to replace cytopores with cheaper microcarriers.

The best suggestion of the present study for cheaper carrier particles is the unicellular microorganisms with spherical and separated cells. These unicellular microorganisms should be photoautotrophic. Photoautotrophs have simple biological requirements, and therefore, their cultivation and proliferation would be less costly. Photoautotrophic unicellular microorganisms include unicellular algae, green sulfur bacteria, and cyanobacteria.

Cyanobacteria are the most needless organisms on the earth, and, because of their simple growth needs, can be used to make valuable medical and commercial products [30]. Therefore, the cultivation and proliferation of cyanobacteria are less expensive than other photoautotrophic unicellular microorganisms.

In Li et al. (2018) CDs and microcarriers were obtained from two separate precursors (citric acid and cytopore) in two separate steps and were finally fixed to each other in the third step [6]. Cyanobacteria, in addition to being a good precursor for making microcarriers, have another advantage, which makes them a good precursor for making CDs. Cyanobacteria produce extracellular polymeric products that mainly include exopolysaccharides (EPS), proteins, lipids, and even DNA, in the form of heteropolymers such as glycoproteins and lipopolysaccharides [30, 31]. Cyanobacterial EPS layer covers the surface of the cells [30]. Neutral sugars such as glucose, xylose, mannose, and arabinose are abundantly found in EPS [30, 32-34]. CDs can be obtained by polymerizing saccharides, citric acid, and amino acids [23]. Therefore, the EPS of cyanobacteria can be a suitable precursor for the production of CDs. Since the EPS layer is attached to cyanobacterial cells, pyrolysis of the cells produces both carrier particles and CDs; also, CDs are fixed on the carrier particles. Thus, all three steps are performed in one, and so it saves time and money.

Traditionally, pyrolysis has been performed by dry or wet pyrolysis methods [35–38]. Pyrolysis of cyanobacteria by dry methods causes the bulk products that have lost their granular nature [35, 36]. Preservation of the granular nature of cyanobacteria cells during pyrolysis is essential, because it affects the efficiency and type of application of product [35]. This technique is known as the microorganism self-template method and is different from the common biotemplating method [35, 39, 40]. Morphological control of carbon-based materials in the form of microspheres can increase their practical efficiency due to good mechanical properties, low flow resistance, high stacking density, and facility in recovering [41–43].

Overall, CDs fabricating methods fall into two general groups: 1- top-down and 2- bottom-up methods [27, 44]. Nowadays, there is a high tendency to extend bottom-up approaches. This high tendency is due to the precise arrangement of molecular precursors, ease of use, cheapness, efficiency, and the simplicity of the methods on the nontoxic precursors [27]. Among the bottom-up methods, hydrothermal is one of the most common methods [27].

To fabricate CDs-loaded carriers (CLCs) from cyanobacteria cells, the pyrolysis process must be done individually and cell-to-cell. For this purpose, each cyanobacteria cell must be pyrolyzed separately and floating. It may seem that the hydrothermal method that is a wet pyrolysis method [35], can be suitable for this purpose because, in this method, pyrolysis is done in water, so it is possible to keep the cells floating and pyrolysis separately. Based on recent studies [35–37] and preliminary tests performed in this study, it was concluded that the hydrothermal method could not give the desired result. Since the EPS is hydrophilic and soluble, the hot water inside the hydrothermal reactor first dissolves them slightly and adheres the cells to each other, and then by a further increase in temperature, all ESP dissolves in water and disperse into it. This process causes the end product to contain a stacked carbon mass without fluorescent properties and a fluorescent solution containing free CDs. Therefore, it was necessary to develop a new method that allows individual pyrolysis of cells with their EPS layers. In this study, a new pyrolysis method called oilothermal was designed for this purpose.

In the oilothermal method, each cell with its hydrous EPS layer is encapsulated by an oil micelle, as if each cell is housed in a micrometric pyrolysis reactor. Also, in this method, a mechanism has been designed to detect the end of the pyrolysis process. Therefore, there is no need to consider an experimental time for pyrolysis. This detection mechanism is based on the volume of gasses produced by pyrolysis.

The oilothermal method can have three advantages over the hydrothermal method: 1- The oil is hydrophobic and does not allow wet cells to stick to each other 2- Oil (frying oil) have a high smoke point so that it can be heated more than 240 °C at atmospheric pressure and so do not require high pressure and the use of a hydrothermal reactor which is an expensive and dangerous device. 3- There is no need to consider an experimental time period for pyrolysis.

It should be noted that the biomass from cyanobacterial blooms, which is considered as bio-pollution in eutrophic aquatic habitats, also can be used for the fabrication of CLCs. This is a win-win approach that has been used in several recent studies to synthesize CDs for bioimaging [45, 46]. However, in the present study, to create the uniform and granular CLCs, a pure culture of Aphanocapsa sp. IBRC-M 5171 has been used. In contrast, in cyanobacterial blooms, a set of species with various shapes are present. However, if uniformity and granularity are not considered about CLCs, cyanobacterial blooms can be a very cheap and available precursor.
Overall, this study aims to develop an efficient method for the fabrication of CLCs from cyanobacteria cells and their characterization in a cadmium-based model.

An active culture of Aphanocapsa sp. IBRC-M 5171 (Cyanobacteria used in the present study) from the Iranian Biological Resource Center was used for proliferation in the bioreactor. The BG-11 medium (IBRESCO, BG11-Broth, IRAN) was used for Aphanocapsa IBRC-M 5171 proliferation. The frying oil (Kourosh food industry, Oila, palm-free frying oil, IRAN) was used in the Oilothermal system. The nitric acid (69%Nitric acid, Merck, Germany) was used to make the eluent solutions. The hydrochloric acid (37%Hydrochloric acid fuming, Merck, Germany) and sodium hydroxide (1310–73–2 Sodium hydroxide CAS, Merck, Germany) were used to make pH adjustment solutions (0.1 м). The 99% acetone (67–64–1 Acetone CAS, Merck, Germany) was used to wash oil.

Under sterile conditions, the active culture of Aphanocapsa sp. IBRC-M 5171 was inoculated into a bioreactor containing 10 L of sterilized BG11 liquid medium. The bioreactor was incubated for one month at 27 °C with a light intensity of 500lux and aerated with cleaned air during this time. After a month, a small amount of cell suspension was extracted from the bioreactor, and its optical density at 730 nm (OD730) was measured by UV / VIS spectrophotometer. OD730 of cell suspension should be less than 0.3200 because, based on many performed tests if the OD730 is less than 0.3200, the cells are better dispersed in the oil. Therefore, the cell suspension must be diluted to reach OD730 <0.3200 before use in the oilothermal system.

The oilothermal system is schematically shown in Fig. 1 . A 250 ml Erlenmeyer flask was used as the pyrolysis chamber and a magnetic stirrer-heater was used for heating. A chrome-coated neodymium rod magnet was used as the stirrer into the pyrolysis chamber, and a piece of hydrophilic cotton was placed at the mouth of the pyrolysis chamber to prevent the drops of condensed water from falling into the oil. To maintain and uniform distribution of heat, the pyrolysis chamber was covered with a ceramic fiber blanket. To measure the temperature, a thermocouple was placed under the ceramic fiber blanket on the body of the pyrolysis chamber and then attached to a digital thermocontroller. Purified steam was used as an oxygen-free atmosphere inside the pyrolysis chamber. To purify the steam, the distilled water was boiled in the boiler for 5 min to remove all dissolved gasses inside it. A silicone hose was attached to the inlet hole of the pyrolysis chamber cap and boiler outlet valve that pure water vapor could enter the pyrolysis chamber and remove and replace the oxygenated air inside it. A mechanism was designed to detect the end of the pyrolysis; that way, a silicone hose was attached to the outlet hole of the pyrolysis chamber and the inlet hole of a 250 ml Erlenmeyer flask. This Erlenmeyer flask was used as a dehumidifier chamber to capture the moisture accumulated inside the tube to prevent any problems with the identification mechanism. A tee was attached to the outlet hole of the humidifier chamber cap, and two valves (valves No. 1 and 2) were attached to the two free ends of it. A rubber hose was attached to one of the valves (valve No. 2) and a capillary tube. The capillary tube was immersed in a test tube containing oil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conversion of cyanobacteria cells to carbon dots-loaded carriers by oilothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: All steps are shown schematically in Fig. 2 . First, 250 ml of the cyanobacterial cells suspension was collected from the bioreactor, passed through a nylon mesh with a pore size of 45 µm, and its OD730 was measured with the spectrophotometer to make sure it was below 0.3200. Then, five times, 50 ml of the cell suspension was poured into a falcon tube and centrifuged with a speed of 4000 rpm for 3 min, and then the supernatant was discarded. To wash the cells, three times, 40 ml distilled water was poured into the falcon tube and placed on a shaker at a speed of 300 rpm for 1 min and again centrifuged at a speed of 4000 rpm for 3 min, and finally, the supernatant was discarded. Then, 50 mL frying oil was poured into the falcon tube, and the cells were suspended in the oil by vortex shake. After the shake, the suspension was poured into a lidded bottle, and an additional 200 ml of oil was added to increase the total amount of oil to 250 ml. Then, the suspension was shaken by a vortex shaker until it became perfectly uniform and without flocculates inside it (To ensure that the cells did not rupture, the oil suspension was reviewed by an optical microscope). After that, the suspension was poured into the pyrolysis chamber (see Fig. 1). To remove oxygenated air inside the pyrolysis chamber, the boiler valve was opened to allow pure water vapor to flow into the pyrolysis chamber to replace the air inside the pyrolysis chamber (see Fig. 1). After the discharge of oxygenated air, the boiler valve was closed. Stirrer rotation speed was set to 3 rps. The temperature was set at 200 °C. To ensure that the cells did not rupture during pyrolysis, the temperature of the oil suspension was increased with a gentle gradient from the initial temperature to 200 °C (25 to 200 °C in 30 min). After the oil suspension has cooled five times, 50 ml of the oil suspension was poured into a falcon tube and centrifuged at a speed of 4000 rpm for 3 min, and then the supernatant was evacuated. To wash the oil from the carbon dots-loaded carriers (CLCs) and make them hydrophilic, three times, 10 ml of the 99% acetone was poured into the falcon tube and placed on a shaker with a speed of 300 rpm for 1 min and again centrifuged with a speed of 4000 rpm for 2 min, and finally, the supernatant was evacuated. After that, 10 ml of distilled water was added, and to remove acetone residue, the suspension was placed into a water bath-Bain Marie at 50 °C for 5 min.
The fresh cyanobacterial cell suspension and the final product (CLCs suspension) are shown in Fig. 3 .
It should be noted that the oil used for pyrolysis can be reused after filtering. Also, the acetone used for washing can be recovered from the supernatant by distillation.

The inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (Agilent, 7900 ICP-Ms, USA) was used to measure cadmium concentrations. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy analysis was performed by the FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nicolet380, USA). The UV-Visible Spectrometry analysis was performed by UV-Visible Spectrometer (GBC Scientific Equipment Ltd., Cintra 101, Australia).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Approval of EPSs as the origin of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To prove that CDs were originated from the pyrolysis of cyanobacteria cells EPSs, it was necessary to prepare free CDs (FCs) and CDs-free carriers (CFCs) suspensions to compare their fluorescent luminosity with each other, and also with fresh cyanobacteria cell suspension, CLCs suspension, frying oil (both fresh and used), and acetone.

To characterize the CDs separately, they must also be obtained from EPSs in pure and free form. For this purpose, 30 mL of cyanobacterial cell suspension with OD730 <0.3200 was prepared. Then EPSs solution was extracted using the lukewarm water method expressed by Ahmed et al. (2014) [47]. The EPSs solution was poured into a hydrothermal autoclave reactor and placed at 200 °C for one hr. After cooling the reactor, the resulting brown solution was collected for analysis.

For this purpose, the EPSs layer was completely eluted from cyanobacterial cells according to the method expressed by Ahmed et al. (2014) [47]. Then, the EPSs-free cells were pyrolyzed by the oilothermal method to obtain the CFCs.

Before fluorescent characterization, to ensure that the CDs were immobilized on the CLCs, the suspension of CLCs was subjected to ultrasonic shaking for 2 min and washed three times with distilled water. Then, fluorescent luminosities of fresh cyanobacteria cell suspension, CLCs suspension, CFCs suspension, FCs suspension, frying oil (both fresh and heated), and acetone were evaluated under UV light (with the wavelength of 228 nm). Also, fluorescence microscope imaging was performed for cyanobacteria cells and CLCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and scanning electron microscopy imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cyanobacteria cells, CLCs, and FCs were evaluated microscopically. Optical microscopy imaging was performed with an optical microscope (Carl Zeiss, Axiostar plus Benchtop Microscope 1169–149 – USED, Germany), and scanning electron microscope (SEM) imaging was performed with FE-SEM (TESCAN, MIRA3, Czech Republic).
To know the structural nature and status of the functional groups, the CLCs were evaluated by the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR).
Adsorption and recovery characterization tests were performed based on Li et al. (2018) [6] with some changes. The tests are shown in detail in Table 1 .
The final test was performed on two certified natural samples including Bu-Ali Sina University Lake water and snow water on the lakeshore.

The FCs, and CLCs suspensions under UV-light, showed similar strong blue-green fluorescent luminosity (Fig. 4 (a), (b)). Of course, the luminosity of CLCs suspensions is slightly blue, which is thought to be due to the Mie scattering of luminosity by CLCs particles. The cyanobacteria cell suspension and frying oil (both fresh and heated) showed a pink, and jadeite-like fluorescent luminosity, respectively (Fig. 4(c), (d), and (e)). The CFCs suspension and acetone did not show fluorescent luminosity (Fig. 4(f) and (g)). Also, the CLCs, and cyanobacteria cells under fluorescence microscope showed strong red and green-yellow fluorescent luminosity, respectively (Fig. 4(h) and (i)). So, it can be said that the fluorescent property of CLCs is due to the EPSs-origin CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and scanning electron microscopy imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Cyanobacteria (Aphanocapsa sp. IBRC-M 5171) cells had a spherical shape with almost similar size (Fig. 5 (a) and (e)). The CLCs and CFCs under the optical microscope were in the form of beads of equal size and rough (Fig. 5(b) and (f)). The roughness and creases of the microsphere surface can be seen with better details in the SEM images (Fig. 5(c),(d),(g), and (h)). Accumulations in the form of velvet balls were seen on the rough surface of the CLCs (Fig. 5(i)). However, these accumulations were not seen on the rough surface of the CFCs (Fig. 5(j)). It appears these accumulations are formed by the interconnection of many smaller nanometric particles. The thick sample of FCs had the same accumulations (Fig. 5(k)). This proves that the accumulations on the rough surface of the pyrolyzed cell are the CDs accumulations.

To size distribution analysis of the FCs, the SEM image of the FCs diluted sample (Fig. 5(l)) was analyzed by Image J software. Based on size distribution analysis, FCs had a mean diameter of 1.16 nm (Fig. 5(m)).

The CLCs FT-IR spectrum was analyzed by the irAnalyze tool from LabCognition software company [48]. The result of the analysis is shown in Fig. 6 . A sharp peak was seen at the 3392.07 cm−1 wavenumber, which is related to the alcoholic OH functional group. In 2082.90 cm−1 wavenumber, a medium stretch was seen that related to N = C = S (Isothiocyanate) functional group. Isothiocyanate is formed by substituting the oxygen in the isocyanate group with sulfur. A sharp peak was seen in 1640.84 cm−1 wavenumber, which is related to the semi-saturated bond of C = C. These semi-saturated bonds of carbon are related to the carbon-rich structure of CLCs. Two weak peaks in 1223.50 and 1098.51 cm−1 wavenumbers were seen related to alkylic CH bent and etheric CO stretch, respectively. Finally, a relatively sharp peak can be seen in the 719.35 cm−1 wavenumber related to the CH rocking.

The pH of the sample has an important effect on the adsorption of heavy metals because it affects both the metal species and the surface charge of the adsorbent [6, 49, 50]. The pH-dependent cadmium adsorption on CLCs was done in the range of pH 1–10. Since at pHs above 10, Qsp of cadmium hydroxide is higher than its Ksp, so cadmium precipitates in the form of cadmium hydroxide, and thus, the evaluation of the adsorption process is futile. The result of the pH-dependent adsorption is shown in Fig. 7 (a). The range of maximum adsorption efficiency (>95%) was extensive and covered the pHs 4 to 10. At pHs <4, the adsorption efficiency decreased due to the increased competition of protons with cadmium to occupy CLCs adsorption sites.

The adsorption kinetics Fig. 7(b)) has the best fit with the pseudo-second-order, and Elovich linear models with r2= 0.9415, and 0.9329, respectively. The Elovich and pseudo-second-order linear models are presented in Eqs. (1) and ((2), respectively. In these equations, qt is the time adsorption capacity (mg g − 1), t is the time (min), α is the initial adsorption rate (mg g − 1 min−1), β is the desorption constant (g mg−1), qe is the equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg g − 1), and k2 is the second-order rate coefficient (g mg−1 min−1).

The adsorption rate was high so that 100% of cadmium was absorbed in 2 min. This high rate of adsorption indicates the high abundance of active sites and the high surface area of CLCs.
(1)
q t
=
β ln
[
α β
]
+ lnt
(2)
t
q t
=
t
q e
+
1
k 2
q
e
2

The adsorption isotherm (Fig. 7(c)) has the best fit to the Langmuir linear model with r2= 0.9947. The Langmuir linear model is shown in Eq. (3) where qe is the equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg g − 1), ce is the adsorbate equilibrium concentration (mg L − 1), qm is the Langmuir constant related to adsorption capacity and ke is the Langmuir constant related to the energy of adsorption.
(3)
c e
q e
=
1
q m
q
k e
+
c e
q m

As can be seen in (Fig. 7(c)), the maximum adsorption capacity was 5305.61 mg kg−1. This is while the contact time was only 2 min. This high adsorption capacity indicates the high abundance of active sites and the high surface area of CLCs.
Dependence of adsorption efficiency on ionic strength and co-existing cations

Ionic strength and co-existing cations have a significant effect on the negative charge balance and the occupancy status of adsorption sites [6]. It was observed that with an increase in ionic strength, the adsorption efficiency decreased (Fig. 8 (a)). Only at concentrations of 0–5 ppm, adsorption efficiency was 100%. So, the maximum tolerance of ionic strength was 5 ppm.

The dependence of adsorption efficiency on some common co-existing cations has been shown in Fig. 8(b). For all co-existing cations, adsorption efficiency from the concentration of 5 ppm onwards reached less than 95%. This reduction in cadmium adsorption efficiency is due to the increase in ionic strength and the competition of co-existing cations with cadmium to occupy CLCs adsorption sites. Overall, the maximum tolerance against ionic strength and these co-existing cations was 5 ppm. So, before using CLCs for solid-phase extraction (SPE) of cadmium in natural samples, the ionic strength, and co-existing cations concentrations should be evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The recovery of cadmium from CLCs
ce:section.ce:para: Dependence of elution efficiency on the eluent concentration

The result of the analysis is shown in Fig. 9 (a). The elution efficiency in the range of 0.1 to 0.3 N HNO3 was almost constant and less than 10%, while after 0.3 N HNO3, the elution efficiency increased with a steep slope and reached more than 90% at 0.5 N HNO3. The elution efficiency constancy in the range of 0.1 to 0.3 N HNO3 can be attributed to the buffering property of CLCs. According to this, the extensive range of maximum pH-dependent elution efficiency can also be attributed to CLCs buffering property, but further studies are needed to prove this hypothesis. Overall, it can be concluded that with increasing eluent concentration, the elution efficiency increases.

In the elution process, some cadmium inside the adsorbent structure may have entered the eluent solution due to minor digestion of the adsorbent and caused an error. But since the concentration of cadmium in the digestion solution of CLCs was zero (Table S1), no error occurred.
Dependence of elution efficiency on the eluent volume

The result of the analysis is shown in Fig. 9(b). when 40 µL of eluent (0.1 N HNO3) was used, the least elution efficiency (5.34%) was obtained. In the range of 80–140 µL, the elution efficiency was almost constant (between 51.87–54.12%), while in the volume of 160 µL, the elution efficiency increased with a steep slope and achieved 97.6%. Here too, the reason for the elution efficiency constancy in the range of 80–140 µL can be attributed to the CLCs buffering property. To prove this hypothesis, further studies are needed. However, about 0.1 N HNO3, elution efficiency increased from 5.34% to 97.6%, when the eluent volume increased from 40 to 160 µL. Overall, it can be concluded that the 160 µL of 0.1 N HNO3 is suitable for the elution of cadmium-loaded CLCs.
Recovery of cadmium in the presence of co-existing cations

Co-existing cations which adsorbed along with cadmium on the CLCs may affect the recovery of cadmium from the adsorbent. So, the recovery of cadmium from the pre-treated CLCs with 2 ppb cadmium solution in a 5 ppm background of co-existing cations was evaluated. Results are shown in Table 2 .
CLCs were used for SPE of trace cadmium in two natural samples. The results are summarized in Table 3 .

Overall, this research innovated a novel carbon dots-loaded carrier that was made from green and a perfect natural precursor. Also, this research developed a new pyrolysis method that, in addition to compatibility with the nature of the cyanobacterial precursor, reduced the fabrication steps, cost, and time. The unique physical and chemical properties of this adsorbent, including high adsorption rate and capacity, efficient use in a wide range of pH, high diversity, and number of functional groups at the surface, made it a potent and efficient adsorbent for SPE of cadmium. Overall, this research has defined a new pathway for the fabricating of carbon dots-loaded carriers that could be a suitable basis for future studies in this field.
The datasets generated during the current study are available at: https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.16757383.v1
Supplementary information is available for this paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We wish to express our sincere gratitude to Professor Mohammad Ali Zolfigol (Professor of Chemistry Bu-Ali Sina University), Professor Dostmorad Zafari and his lab team (Mycology and plant pathology, Bu-Ali Sina University), Professor Gholam Khodakaramian (Professor of Plant Pathogenic Prokaryotes, Bu-Ali Sina University), Professor Abdolkarim Chehregani Rad (Professor of Cell and Developmental Biology, Bu-Ali Sina University) and Professor Ali Akbar Safari Sinegani (Professor of Soil Biology, Bu-Ali Sina University) for their contribution to the execution of this study.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2022.104210.
